file_name: Karabulut et al. - 2022 - Deep learning as a new tool in the diagnosis of mycosis fungoides.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning for nuclei detection in Hematoxylin-Eosin stained micrographs of Mycosis Fungoides (a cutaneous T-cell lymphoma) and machine learning classifiers (Multi-layer Perceptron and Decision Tree) to detect lymphocytes and classify MF versus non-MF specimens.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as an 'ORIGINAL PAPER' and presents original experimental work involving deep learning models for nuclei detection and classification of Mycosis Fungoides. It includes specific sections for 'Materials and methods' (describing data collection from 11 patients and 66 micrographs), 'Results' (reporting statistical comparisons and classifier performance metrics like 90.5% and 94.2% prediction power), and 'Discussion', which are characteristic of primary research studies rather than reviews.

title: Deep learning as a new tool in the diagnosis of mycosis fungoides
brief_summary: This study developed a deep learning-based computer-aided diagnosis tool to differentiate Mycosis Fungoides (MF) from non-MF inflammatory skin conditions using Hematoxylin-Eosin stained micrographs. The researchers analyzed 66 micrographs from 11 patients, employing a deep learning model for nuclei detection and extracting 10 geometric and chromatic nuclear features. A Multi-layer Perceptron classifier was used to detect lymphocytes with 90.5% prediction power, while a Decision Tree classifier distinguished MF from non-MF lymphocytes with 94.2% prediction power. Statistical analysis revealed that MF nuclei were significantly smaller, darker, and more heterogeneous than non-MF nuclei. The results suggest that this computational approach can enhance diagnostic accuracy and objectivity for MF, potentially serving as a valuable adjunct to pathologists.

lesion_types_studied.details: Primary target class; malignant cutaneous T-cell lymphoma. Studied in 4 patients (early stage I and IIA). MF lymphocytes were distinguished from non-MF lymphocytes using a Decision Tree classifier with 94.2% prediction power.
Comparator class for MF. Included 7 patients with non-MF pathology, specifically spongiotic dermatitis. Used to differentiate MF from inflammatory conditions.

all_task_types.target: Lymphocyte detection (lymphocyte vs. non-lymphocyte) and Mycosis Fungoides (MF) vs. non-MF classification.
all_task_types.details: The study employed two distinct classification tasks. First, a Multi-layer Perceptron (MLP) classifier was used to detect lymphocytes among detected nuclei (binary classification: lymphocyte vs. non-lymphocyte) with 90.5% prediction power. Second, a Decision Tree classifier was used to distinguish MF lymphocytes from non-MF lymphocytes (binary classification: MF vs. non-MF) with 94.2% prediction power. The models predict discrete diagnostic categories based on extracted nuclear features.

datasets_used.name: Mersin University Archival Hematoxylin-Eosin Micrograph Dataset
datasets_used.description: A private dataset consisting of 66 Hematoxylin-Eosin (H-E) stained micrographs obtained from archival paraffin-embedded biopsies diagnosed at Mersin University. The images were captured using an Olympus DP27 digital microscope camera at 200x magnification. The dataset includes specimens from 11 patients: 4 with early-stage (I and IIA) Mycosis Fungoides (MF) and 7 with non-MF inflammatory skin conditions (specifically spongiotic dermatitis).

use.training: The 66 micrographs were used to generate nuclei and lymphocyte features. For the lymphocyte detection task (MLP), 11,429 nuclei from 18 micrographs were used. For the MF classification task (Decision Tree), 6,788 lymphocytes (4,009 MF and 2,779 non-MF) were analyzed. The study utilized a 20%/80% test-training split for both classifiers, with 100 random test-train split runs performed.
use.validation: Not explicitly reported as a separate hold-out set; validation was performed via 100 random test-train split runs.
use.testing: Used in the 20%/80% test-training split for evaluating the Multi-layer Perceptron (90.5% prediction power) and Decision Tree (94.2% prediction power) classifiers.
use.other: The dataset was also used for statistical analysis comparing 10 nuclear properties (area, perimeter, roundness, hue, saturation, brightness, and their heterogeneities) between MF and non-MF groups.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To test each feature for both groups (non-MF and MF) for normality.
To compare all 10 nuclear properties between MF and non-MF groups, as all groups were found to be normally distributed.
To estimate the uncertainty of the average prediction power for the lymphocyte detection algorithm (90.5%) and the MF detection algorithm (94.2%) based on 100 random test-train split runs.

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no difference in the mean values of the 10 nuclear properties (area, perimeter, roundness, hue, saturation, brightness, and their heterogeneities) between MF and non-MF lymphocytes.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a statistically significant difference in the mean values of the 10 nuclear properties between MF and non-MF lymphocytes.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: All 10 parameters were found to be statistically significantly different between MF and non-MF groups, indicating that the null hypothesis was rejected for all tested nuclear properties.

lymphocyte_detection.notes: The MLP classifier was chosen with alpha parameter and L2 penalty (regularization term) set as 0.01. It yielded maximum predictive power. The model had a lower false positive ratio compared to other classifiers tested, with a reasonable missed lymphocyte ratio.

Decision Tree.features_used: Saturation average, brightness average, and prediction score of nuclei detection algorithm

mf_vs_non_mf_classification.notes: The Decision Tree classifier was the best performing classifier among those tried from the scikit-learn library. Significant predictive power was achieved using only the best performing 3 features.

Student's t-test.result: All 10 parameters were statistically significantly different between MF and non-MF groups (p < 0.05 implied by significance)

statistical_comparison_of_nuclear_proper.notes: MF nuclei were found to be smaller, darker, and more heterogeneous than non-MF nuclei. All groups for each of the 10 features and 2 classes were normally distributed.

architecture_used.main_model: Multi-layer Perceptron (MLP) for lymphocyte detection; Decision Tree for Mycosis Fungoides (MF) vs. non-MF classification. Nuclei detection was performed using an open-source algorithm (referenced as Mask R-CNN in citations [5, 6]).
architecture_used.input_size: Original images were 2448 × 1920 pixels, divided into 600 × 600 pixels sized patches for nuclei detection.

nuclei_detection.model_type: Open-source algorithm (Mask R-CNN based on citations)
nuclei_detection.output: Prediction scores, binary masks, and region of interest (minimum enclosing box)

lymphocyte_detection.features_used: 10 nuclear properties (geometric: area, perimeter, roundness; chromatic: hue, saturation, brightness, and their heterogeneities)

mf_classification.features_used: Saturation average, brightness average, and prediction score of nuclei detection algorithm

lymphocyte_detection.preprocessing: Feature standardization to standard normal distribution

mf_classification.preprocessing: Feature standardization to standard normal distribution

preprocessing_techniques: Image patching: Original images (2448 × 1920 pixels) were divided into 600 × 600 pixel patches for nuclei detection.
Feature standardization: Nuclear features were standardized to a standard normal distribution using scikit-learn methods before classification.
Outlier exclusion: Outliers resulting from detection failures (e.g., multiple nuclei detected as one) were excluded during the analysis stage.
conclusions: Deep learning and machine learning models can effectively differentiate Mycosis Fungoides (MF) from non-MF inflammatory skin conditions using Hematoxylin-Eosin stained micrographs.

The Multi-layer Perceptron classifier achieved an average 90.5% prediction power for lymphocyte detection, while the Decision Tree classifier achieved an average 94.2% prediction power for distinguishing MF from non-MF lymphocytes.
MF nuclei were found to be statistically significantly smaller, darker, and more heterogeneous in hue and brightness compared to non-MF nuclei.
The study challenges traditionally defined nuclear properties of MF by demonstrating that MF lymphocytes are smaller rather than larger, contrary to some previous literature.
The computational approach offers objectivity and data abundance that can enhance diagnostic accuracy and potentially serve as a valuable adjunct to pathologists in diagnosing MF.
The developed models have the potential to make MF diagnoses easier, more accurate, and earlier, bridging the gap between computational advancement and medical practice.
important_remarks: The study relies on a very small, single-center dataset of only 66 micrographs from 11 patients (4 MF, 7 non-MF), limiting generalizability and increasing the risk of dataset bias.
The dataset is heavily imbalanced, with 4,009 MF lymphocytes compared to 2,779 non-MF lymphocytes, which may skew classifier performance.
The study lacks external validation; models were evaluated using only 100 random test-train splits on the internal dataset, raising concerns about overfitting and robustness.
The findings challenge established pathological literature by reporting that MF nuclei are smaller and darker than non-MF nuclei, contrary to traditional descriptions of enlarged nuclei.
The lymphocyte detection algorithm exhibited a high missed lymphocyte ratio (24%), indicating significant sensitivity issues in identifying true lymphocytes.
The study utilized archival, fixed paraffin-embedded biopsies rather than fresh or whole-slide imaging, which may introduce staining and morphological artifacts affecting feature extraction.

The nuclei detection algorithm was an open-source implementation (Mask R-CNN) not specifically trained or validated for this specific dermatological context, potentially introducing domain mismatch errors.
The study did not report performance metrics for the nuclei detection stage itself, only for the downstream lymphocyte and MF classification stages.
